one_line_takeaway: CIPL reframes agent privacy leakage as a channel-inversion measurement problem rather than a memory-specific attack, showing empirically across five LLM providers that memory leakage is near-saturated (CER/AER ~1.0) while retrieval- and tool-mediated leakage form distinct frequent-but-partial and channel/provider-dependent regimes, and that exact-match extraction metrics alone (missing 12/200 semantically-leaked samples) undercount real privacy risk.
key_finding: Three cross-target risk regimes emerge under the shared protocol. (1) Memory is a near-saturated high-risk special case: both memory_ehr and memory_rap reach CER = AER = 1.0000 (+/-0.0000) across all five providers. (2) Retrieval-mediated leakage (rag_ctrl) is frequent-but-partial: MiniMax-M2.5 gets CER=0.2133, AER=0.9533; MiniMax-M2.7 gets CER=0.2000, AER=0.9800; qwen3.5-plus, DeepSeek and GPT-4o all show CER=0.0 with AER=0.8000 -- i.e. leakage is common but rarely complete. (3) Tool-mediated leakage (tool_ctrl, LLM-in-the-loop) is channel- and provider-dependent: return_echo generally beats args_exfil away from provider ceiling effects (e.g. MiniMax-M2.5 AER rises from 0.2800 to 0.3667; MiniMax-M2.7 from 0.2667 to 0.3733; qwen3.5-plus from 0.3067 to 0.4000), but DeepSeek is near-saturated on both channels (args CER=1.0000, echo CER=0.9933) and GPT-4o shows high AER (0.9933) but lower CER (0.4867) for args_exfil versus CER=1.0 for return_echo -- so the ordering is not universal. Cleaned weak-control prompts sharply suppress leakage (e.g. args_exfil AER falls to 0.0267/0.0133/0/0/0 for the five providers respectively; return_echo AER falls to 0.0667/0.0067/0/0/0.0200), showing the main-experiment leakage is attack-induced rather than an artifact of ordinary completion. Increasing retrieval depth k on rag_ctrl does not monotonically increase complete extraction: at k=4, MiniMax-M2.5 AER=1.0000 but CER drops to 0.0556, and MiniMax-M2.7 shows AER=0.9889 with CER=0.0222, while qwen3.5-plus/DeepSeek/GPT-4o stay roughly stable around AER 0.8 across depths. The semantic annotation study (200 hand-labeled samples) finds semantic_AER=0.5000 and semantic_CER=0.4400, with 12 samples labeled exact=0 & semantic=1 (no exact-match unit recovered, yet semantically recoverable sensitive content present), 88 samples exact=1 & semantic=2 (fully informative under both), and 100 samples exact=0 & semantic=0 (no leakage under either criterion) -- demonstrating that exact-match extraction alone can undercount attacker-useful privacy risk.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (note: the arXiv PDF title is 'Observable Channels, Not Just Storage: Evaluating Privacy Leakage in LLM Agent Pipelines', while the record's listed title 'CIPL: A Target-Independent Framework for Channel-Inversion Privacy Leakage in Agents' names the same CIPL framework) is CIPL (Channel Inversion for Privacy Leakage), a unified channel-oriented measurement interface for evaluating privacy leakage across heterogeneous LLM agent pipelines. It represents any target by a shared 'target signature' tau = (S, Sel, Asm, Exec, Obs, Ext) over sensitive source, selection, assembly, execution, observation and extraction stages, and represents an attack instance as a = (locator, aligner, diversification policy). Using this shared interface it instantiates and empirically compares memory-based, retrieval-mediated, and tool-mediated leakage targets under one protocol and metric vocabulary (RN, EN, EE, CER, AER). The authors explicitly state CIPL is a measurement/evaluation interface, not a claim of a universally strongest attack recipe, and that memory extraction (from prior work, MEXTRA, Wang et al. 2025) is recovered as one special case of the general formulation rather than the paper's novel attack.
threat_model: Black-box attacker who interacts with the target agent pipeline only through user-visible inputs (no access to model weights, hidden states, or privileged internal logs). Two knowledge levels are defined: a 'basic' setting where the attacker knows only coarse information (application domain, task type), and an 'advanced' setting where the attacker may additionally infer limited implementation cues (e.g., whether selection is lexically or semantically sensitive). The attacker submits an attack budget of n queries and seeks to maximize the union of recoverable leaked units across trials; this is explicitly distinguished from generic jailbreak/prompt-injection objectives -- the goal is exposing private internal dependence through an attacker-visible channel (final answer text, structured evidence fields, tool-call arguments, or echoed tool-return content), not inducing arbitrary misbehavior.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) the target set is controlled rather than exhaustive -- the retrieval- and tool-mediated targets are designed to isolate channel realization under a shared protocol, not to reproduce the full complexity of deployed agent stacks, so the paper does not claim to have measured all privacy risk in real-world RAG/tool ecosystems. (2) Semantic privacy risk is only partially observed -- the 200-sample semantic annotation is not large enough to support fine-grained target-wise or provider-wise semantic breakdowns, and closed-weight API providers introduce residual variability from backend updates, nondeterminism, and provider-specific formatting despite standardized prompts and fixed budgets. (3) The paper is measurement-first, not defense-complete -- it shows cleaned weak controls and defense-style prompting can suppress leakage on some channels but does not benchmark a broader mitigation suite under the same protocol, nor evaluate richer deployment surfaces such as logs, traces, or human-review interfaces.

datasets_used: EHRAgent-derived memory_ehr target (source size 200, top-k=4 retrieval)
RAP-style web-agent-derived memory_rap target (source size 200, retrieval depth k=3)
rag_ctrl: controlled compact document store (source size 5, default retrieval depth k=2)
tool_ctrl: controlled tool-mediated workflow with args_exfil and return_echo observation modes (source size 5, default retrieval depth k=2)
200 manually annotated output samples for the semantic leakage study
quantitative_results: memory_ehr and memory_rap: CER = AER = 1.0000 +/- 0.0000 across all 5 providers (MiniMax-M2.5, MiniMax-M2.7, qwen3.5-plus, DeepSeek, GPT-4o)

tool_ctrl args_exfil (LLM-in-the-loop): MiniMax-M2.5 CER=0.2600, AER=0.2800; MiniMax-M2.7 CER=0.2667, AER=0.2667; qwen3.5-plus CER=AER=0.3067 (sd 0.3486); DeepSeek CER=AER=1.0000; GPT-4o CER=0.4867, AER=0.9933
tool_ctrl return_echo (LLM-in-the-loop): MiniMax-M2.5 CER=0.3600, AER=0.3667; MiniMax-M2.7 CER=0.3667, AER=0.3733; qwen3.5-plus CER=AER=0.4000; DeepSeek CER=0.9933, AER=1.0000; GPT-4o CER=AER=1.0000
Cleaned weak-control AER for args_exfil: 0.0267 (MiniMax-M2.5), 0.0133 (MiniMax-M2.7), 0.0000 (qwen3.5-plus, DeepSeek, GPT-4o)
RAG retrieval-depth ablation at k=4: MiniMax-M2.5 AER=1.0000 but CER=0.0556; MiniMax-M2.7 AER=0.9889, CER=0.0222; qwen3.5-plus/DeepSeek/GPT-4o remain around AER~0.8

Semantic annotation study (n=200 labeled samples): semantic_AER=0.5000, semantic_CER=0.4400; exact=0 & semantic=1 in 12 samples; exact=1 & semantic=2 in 88 samples; exact=0 & semantic=0 in 100 samples
All main experiments: attack budget n=30 queries, 1 retry, 5 seeds {0,1,2,3,4}; execution_error_trials = 0.0000 in every reported row
baselines_compared: MEXTRA-style memory extraction attack (Wang et al. 2025) -- recovered as a special case of CIPL's general formulation, not re-proposed as novel by this paper
Naive/main prompt family vs. cleaned weak-control prompts (ablation to isolate attack-induced vs. baseline completion leakage)
Deterministic vs. LLM-in-the-loop variants of tool_ctrl (channel-level upper bound vs. realized leakage)
Appendix F: comparison of the full locator-aligner-diversification attack construction against naive baselines, shown to not be uniformly dominant across all targets
